headline: Energy Surges as Geopolitical Heat Scorches Software; Wabtec Steals the Show
body_html:
Wednesday's session closed with a clear fault line running through the market: commodities-adjacent names advanced sharply while software stocks bore the brunt of the selling. Breadth was negative — 271 decliners outpaced 220 advancers in our universe — yet the divergence between sectors told a more nuanced story than a simple risk-off day. Escalating tensions around the Strait of Hormuz, fresh U.S. military posturing toward Iranian infrastructure, and reports of additional vessels rerouting away from the Red Sea combined to push energy to the top of the leaderboard, with the sector gaining 1.63% on the session.

Rail-equipment supplier Wabtec was the unambiguous standout mover of the day, surging 10.04% to claim the top spot among gainers. Natural-gas producers EQT Corp and Expand Energy added 8.45% and 5.18% respectively, riding the commodity tailwind. Materials and homebuilders also edged higher. On the other side of the ledger, software endured a bruising session, sliding 2.11%, with enterprise-software name Pegasystems collapsing 16.0% and several cloud-adjacent peers posting steep losses as well. Healthcare fell 1.30%.

The session's takeaway is that the market is pricing a world where energy supply routes remain contested and defense budgets stay elevated — a backdrop that rewards hard assets and penalizes growth multiples. With the World Bank chief economist warning that an escalating Middle East conflict could drag global growth to 1.3% this year, investors appear to be repositioning rather than simply hedging. Watch whether today's sector rotation holds into Thursday's open.

briefs.headline: Wabtec Rockets 10% After Earnings Impress
briefs.body: Rail-equipment maker Wabtec surged 10.04% Wednesday, the largest single-day gain in our 500-name universe, as the session's broader industrial sell-off made the move all the more conspicuous. The company's results appear to have caught the market off-guard, drawing outsized buying volume through the close.

briefs.headline: Pegasystems Hammered 16% in Enterprise-Software Rout
briefs.body: Pegasystems led all decliners, cratering 16.0% in a session that was already unkind to software broadly. The enterprise-software sector shed 2.11% on the day, with N-able off nearly 9% and Twilio dropping 6.54%, suggesting the selling pressure was thematic rather than confined to a single name.

briefs.headline: Hormuz Threats Lift Energy as Tanker Routes Shift
briefs.body: President Trump's pledge to strike Iranian infrastructure for every vessel targeted in the Strait of Hormuz sent energy names sharply higher. Shipping-data trackers confirmed that more vessels are altering Red Sea routes in response to Houthi threats, tightening the perceived supply outlook and fueling gains across natural-gas producers.

briefs.headline: World Bank Sounds Alarm on Middle East Escalation Risk
briefs.body: The World Bank's chief economist warned Wednesday that a broadening Middle East conflict could compress global economic growth to just 1.3% in 2026. The stark projection added a macro dimension to market anxiety, reinforcing the defensive rotation into energy and hard assets evident throughout the session.

briefs.headline: GE Vernova Slides 8.7% Despite Power-Sector Tailwinds
briefs.body: GE Vernova shed 8.69% Wednesday, one of the steeper declines among large industrials, even as broader energy themes lifted commodity names. The power-equipment company's drop dragged on the industrials sector, which finished down 0.13% and stood in stark contrast to gains posted in energy and materials.

briefs.headline: Senate Panel Advances Bill That Could Restrict Foreign Auto Access
briefs.body: A Senate committee moved forward with legislation that would restrict Chinese-linked auto technology from the U.S. market, with language that could also affect European manufacturers with Chinese-production ties. The bill adds to a growing list of trade-policy uncertainties weighing on global industrial supply chains heading into the second half of 2026.
